segments.text: Opening shot. Overpass Turbo gives us a map, an editor, and a city-sized question.
segments.fallback_text: Opening shot: Overpass Turbo, map ready, editor waiting.
segments.subtitle: Step 1: Open Overpass Turbo and load the map + query editor workspace

segments.text: Tiny dramatic pause here. Even cartography likes a movie beat.
segments.fallback_text: Quick pause here while the map catches up.
segments.subtitle: Tiny dramatic pause here. Even cartography likes a movie beat.

segments.text: This framing helps immediately. Logic on one side, evidence on the other.
segments.fallback_text: Editor left, map right. Nice.
segments.subtitle: Step 2: Split view: query editor on left, map + controls on right

segments.text: Our first question is simple. Show us hospitals across Hyderabad, and keep the search area explicit.
segments.fallback_text: First question for the city: show us the hospitals in Hyderabad.
segments.subtitle: Step 3: Write a Hyderabad hospitals query in Overpass QL using a fixed city bounding box

segments.text: I think it is still loading, so we let the map finish the thought.
segments.fallback_text: I think it is loading, so we wait for the reveal.
segments.subtitle: I think it is still loading, so we let the map finish the thought.

segments.text: Now we run it. This is the part where the city answers back.
segments.fallback_text: And... run. Now we let the map do its detective work.
segments.subtitle: Step 4: Run query to load all mapped hospitals as explorable markers

segments.text: Hmm... the map is thinking for a second, which feels fair.
segments.fallback_text: Hmm... the map is still thinking. Let's give it a second.
segments.subtitle: Hmm... the map is thinking for a second, which feels fair.

segments.text: Instead of hunting around the map, we jump straight to where the results live.
segments.fallback_text: There we go, zoom straight to the results.
segments.subtitle: Step 5: Use 'zoom to data' to jump map viewport directly onto Hyderabad results

segments.text: A marker turns the dots into actual places, with names, tags, and context.
segments.fallback_text: Let's tap a marker and see the story behind the pin.
segments.subtitle: Step 6: Click map markers to inspect feature tags like name, address, and amenity type

segments.text: Data view is the reality check. The map looks nice, but the table tells the truth.
segments.fallback_text: Quick detour into Data view... this is the raw evidence.
segments.subtitle: Step 7: Open Data tab to inspect raw tabular output and OSM attributes

segments.text: Then we go back to the map, because spatial patterns are easier to feel than to read.
segments.fallback_text: Back to the map. Easier to read.
segments.subtitle: Step 8: Switch back to Map view to visually compare coverage across neighborhoods

segments.text: Same workflow, new question. This time we ask about charging stations instead of hospitals.
segments.fallback_text: Round two: same rhythm, new question... where are the charging stations?
segments.subtitle: Step 9: Replace with EV charging station query to analyze clean-mobility readiness

segments.text: Tiny dramatic pause here. Even cartography likes a movie beat.
segments.fallback_text: Quick pause here while the map catches up.
segments.subtitle: Tiny dramatic pause here. Even cartography likes a movie beat.

segments.text: Now the comparison gets interesting. Different services, same city, one visual frame.
segments.fallback_text: Results are coming in. A little pause here while the map connects the dots.
segments.subtitle: Step 10: EV charging points rendered; now compare spread versus healthcare locations

segments.text: Looks like the map wants a second cup of coffee, so we wait.
segments.fallback_text: Even the map wants a little breath here.
segments.subtitle: Looks like the map wants a second cup of coffee, so we wait.

segments.text: Next layer: schools. The story gets better when we ask the city more than one question.
segments.fallback_text: Next scene: schools. Same city, new layer of the story.
segments.subtitle: Step 11: Run school query to map education infrastructure in the same city boundary

segments.text: I think it is still loading, so we let the map finish the thought.
segments.fallback_text: I think it is loading, so we wait for the reveal.
segments.subtitle: I think it is still loading, so we let the map finish the thought.

segments.text: Now pharmacies join in, and the map starts feeling less like a query result and more like a city portrait.
segments.fallback_text: Now pharmacies join the cast, and the city starts to feel complete.
segments.subtitle: Step 12: Add pharmacy query as a fourth dataset to build a richer city-services comparison

segments.text: Hmm... the map is thinking for a second, which feels fair.

segments.text: This is the quiet win. One editor lets us iterate across datasets without changing tools or losing context.
segments.fallback_text: One editor, many datasets... that is the part I really like.
segments.subtitle: Step 13: Single editor workflow lets you iterate quickly across multiple urban datasets

segments.text: Tiny dramatic pause here. Even cartography likes a movie beat.
segments.fallback_text: Quick pause here while the map catches up.
segments.subtitle: Tiny dramatic pause here. Even cartography likes a movie beat.

segments.text: Once the map makes sense, export matters. A good demo should hand off cleanly to the next workflow.
segments.fallback_text: Open export. Now this can travel.
segments.subtitle: Step 14: Open Export options for downstream GIS and analytics workflows

segments.text: GeoJSON is the practical handoff here. Easy to carry into GIS tools, code, or a web map.
segments.fallback_text: GeoJSON, please. Clean handoff.
segments.subtitle: Step 15: Export supports GeoJSON, KML, GPX, and raw OSM for different tools

segments.text: Final shot. We went from query editing to clean, exportable geo-data.
segments.fallback_text: That is the wrap.
segments.subtitle: Step 16: Tutorial complete: from iterative query editing to exportable, reproducible geo-data
